name: The truth of suffering
body:
"Birth is suffering, aging is suffering, illness is suffering, death is suffering. Sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair are suffering. Union with what is displeasing is suffering; separation from what is pleasing is suffering; not getting what one wants is suffering. In short, the five aggregates subject to clinging are suffering." (SN 56.11)

This is not pessimism. It is naming what is. The Buddha began here because no one walks a path until they admit they need one. Dukkha is not only the obvious sufferings — pain, loss, illness — but also the subtler dukkha of getting what you wanted and then watching it slip away, and the deepest dukkha of being a self at all in a world that will not stay still for you.

The first truth asks one thing: stop pretending. Look honestly at what your life actually contains.

name: The truth of the origin
body:
"This is the noble truth of the origin of suffering: it is this craving (taṇhā) which gives rise to renewed existence, accompanied by delight and lust, finding delight here and there — namely, craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for non-existence." (SN 56.11)

The second truth is the diagnosis. Suffering does not arrive from outside. It is generated, in each moment, by the mind that wants — wants this experience to continue, wants that experience to end, wants to be someone else, wants the world to be other than it is. Craving and the dukkha it produces are not separate things. They are two views of one process.

This is also where the Buddha's teaching becomes radically practical. If suffering had an external cause, you could only escape it by escaping the world. Because suffering has an internal cause, freedom is possible from where you sit.

name: The truth of cessation
body:
"This is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering: it is the remainderless fading away and cessation of that same craving, the giving up and relinquishing of it, freedom from it, non-reliance on it." (SN 56.11)

The third truth is the most easily missed. The Buddha is not saying "suffering can be reduced" or "suffering can be reframed." He is saying that there is a state in which the cause of suffering — craving itself — has ceased, and where craving has ceased, suffering has ceased. He spoke from direct experience, not theory.

This is why the path is worth walking. The third truth is the reason the fourth exists. Without the third, the fourth is just self-improvement. With it, the fourth is liberation.

name: The truth of the path
short: A way to the end exists — the Eightfold Path.
body:
"This is the noble truth of the way leading to the cessation of suffering: it is this Noble Eightfold Path — that is, right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration." (SN 56.11)

The fourth truth contains everything practical the Buddha taught. The other three are diagnosis. The fourth is the prescription. Every reflection in this app, every sutta you read, every sit you take, every ethical choice you make — all of it lives inside this fourth truth.

The eight branches are not steps to complete in sequence. They are eight dimensions of one path, developed together. You don't perfect right view first and then move on to right speech. The Buddha was emphatic about this in MN 117 (the Mahācattārīsaka Sutta): the eight support each other simultaneously. Right view orients right intention. Right intention shapes right speech. Right speech and action build the steady mind that makes right concentration possible. Right concentration deepens right view. The whole thing turns together, like a wheel.
